title: AI Video Creation Showdown
description: Create a short video using the top free AI video tools. Compare quality, speed, and ease of use side by side.

steps.title: Pick a simple prompt
steps.instructions: Choose a short, visual prompt like: 'A golden retriever running through autumn leaves in slow motion'. Use the same prompt for all tools to compare fairly.
steps.tip: Keep it simple — complex prompts reveal differences but simple ones show baseline quality.

steps.title: Try Runway (free credits)
steps.instructions: Go to runwayml.com, sign up for a free account. Click 'Generate' > 'Text to Video'. Paste your prompt. Select Gen-4 if available. Generate and download.
steps.tip: You get 125 free credits — enough for a few short clips.

steps.title: Try Kling (free daily credits)
steps.instructions: Go to klingai.com, create a free account. Use 'Text to Video' mode. Paste the same prompt. Generate and download.
steps.tip: Kling gives daily free credits that reset — come back tomorrow for more.

steps.title: Try Pika (free daily generations)
steps.instructions: Go to pika.art, sign up for free. Paste the same prompt. Generate and download.
steps.tip: Pika is the fastest — great for quick social media clips.

steps.title: Compare results
steps.instructions: Play all three videos side by side. Note: Which looks most realistic? Which has the best motion? Which was fastest? Which had the easiest interface?
steps.tip: Save your favorites — these tools improve monthly, so re-test in 4-6 weeks.

expectedOutcome: Three short AI-generated videos to compare. You'll have a clear sense of which tool suits your needs and budget.

title: Chat AI Head-to-Head: ChatGPT vs Claude vs Gemini
description: Test the top 3 AI chatbots with the same prompts to see which fits your style. All have free tiers.

steps.title: Prepare test prompts
steps.instructions: Write 3 test prompts: (1) A creative task: 'Write a short story about a time traveler who can only go back 5 minutes', (2) An analysis task: 'Compare the pros and cons of remote work vs office work', (3) A practical task: 'Create a weekly meal plan for a family of 4 on a $150 CAD budget'.
steps.tip: Use prompts that matter to YOU — you'll get a much better sense of which tool fits.

steps.title: Test ChatGPT
steps.instructions: Go to chat.openai.com, sign up or log in (free). Send all 3 prompts in separate conversations. Save or screenshot the responses.
steps.tip: ChatGPT tends to be more verbose — great for brainstorming.

steps.title: Test Claude
steps.instructions: Go to claude.ai, sign up or log in (free). Send the same 3 prompts. Save responses.
steps.tip: Claude tends to be more nuanced and careful — great for analysis.

steps.title: Test Gemini
steps.instructions: Go to gemini.google.com, log in with Google account (free). Send the same 3 prompts. Save responses.
steps.tip: Gemini can search the web in real-time — great for current topics.

steps.title: Pick your favorite
steps.instructions: Compare all 9 responses (3 prompts x 3 tools). Which gave the best creative writing? Best analysis? Most practical advice? Note your overall preference.
steps.tip: You don't have to pick just one — many people use different tools for different tasks.

expectedOutcome: Clear understanding of which AI chatbot matches your communication style and needs. You may find each excels at different tasks.

title: Create Your First AI Song
description: Generate a full original song with lyrics and vocals using Suno's free tier. No musical knowledge needed.

toolsNeeded.role: AI music generator — creates full songs from text

hardwareNeeded: Computer with web browser and speakers/headphones

steps.title: Sign up for Suno
steps.instructions: Go to suno.com and create a free account. You get 10 free songs per day.

steps.title: Create with a simple prompt
steps.instructions: Click 'Create'. In the text box, describe your song: 'An upbeat pop song about starting a new adventure, with catchy chorus'. Click Generate.
steps.tip: Suno generates 2 variations — listen to both!

steps.title: Try custom mode
steps.instructions: Click 'Create' again, toggle 'Custom' mode. Now you can write your own lyrics, choose a genre (e.g., 'indie folk', 'electronic', 'hip hop'), and set the mood.
steps.tip: Try different genres with the same lyrics to hear how dramatically the output changes.

steps.title: Download and share
steps.instructions: Find your favorite generation, click the download button. Share with friends or keep for yourself!
steps.tip: Free tier songs are for personal use only. Pro ($14 CAD/mo) allows commercial use.

expectedOutcome: A complete original song with lyrics, vocals, and instrumentation — created in minutes with no musical training.

title: Build a Web Page with AI (No Coding Required)
description: Use an AI coding assistant to create a complete web page from a plain English description. See how AI handles code generation.

toolsNeeded.role: Generate the complete web page code

hardwareNeeded: Computer with web browser and a text editor (Notepad works)

steps.title: Describe what you want
steps.instructions: Go to claude.ai (free). Type: 'Create a complete HTML page for a personal recipe collection. Include a nice design with categories for Breakfast, Lunch, and Dinner. Add 2 example recipes per category with ingredients and instructions. Make it look professional with modern CSS styling. Put everything in a single HTML file.'
steps.tip: The more specific you are, the better the result.

steps.title: Copy the code
steps.instructions: Claude will generate a complete HTML file. Click the 'Copy' button on the code block.

steps.title: Save and open
steps.instructions: Open Notepad (or any text editor). Paste the code. Save as 'recipes.html' (make sure to change 'Save as type' to 'All Files'). Double-click the saved file to open it in your browser.
steps.tip: If it doesn't look right, go back to Claude and say 'The layout is broken, can you fix it?' — Claude can iterate.

steps.title: Customize with AI
steps.instructions: Go back to Claude and ask for changes: 'Add a dark mode toggle' or 'Replace the example recipes with Italian dishes' or 'Add a search bar'. Copy the updated code and replace the file.
steps.tip: This is exactly how this AI Progress Dashboard was built!

expectedOutcome: A professional-looking web page created entirely by AI, running locally in your browser. You'll see firsthand how AI coding assistants work.

title: AI Image Battle: Free vs Paid
description: Compare free and paid AI image generators with the same prompt. See if the paid options are worth it for your needs.

steps.title: Choose a test prompt
steps.instructions: Use something visually specific: 'A cozy coffee shop on a rainy evening, warm lights glowing through the windows, watercolor painting style'.
steps.tip: Including a style ('watercolor', 'photorealistic', 'comic book') helps the AI produce better results.

steps.title: Try Craiyon (free, no signup)
steps.instructions: Go to craiyon.com. Paste the prompt and click Draw. Wait ~60 seconds. Save the best result.
steps.tip: Craiyon is slower and lower quality, but completely free with no limits — great for brainstorming.

steps.title: Try DALL-E via ChatGPT (free tier)
steps.instructions: Go to chat.openai.com (free account). Type 'Generate an image: [your prompt]'. ChatGPT will create an image. Download it.
steps.tip: Free tier has daily image limits. If you hit the limit, try tomorrow.

steps.title: Try Adobe Firefly (25 free credits)
steps.instructions: Go to firefly.adobe.com, create a free Adobe account. Click 'Text to Image'. Paste the prompt. Generate and download.
steps.tip: Firefly images are safe for commercial use — trained only on licensed content.

steps.title: Compare and decide
steps.instructions: Put all images side by side. Consider: quality, style accuracy, speed, and whether the free options meet your needs or if you'd want to try Midjourney ($14 CAD/mo).
steps.tip: For most casual use, free tools are great. Midjourney is worth trying if you need consistent, high-quality artistic images.

expectedOutcome: Side-by-side comparison of free AI image generators. You'll know whether free tools meet your needs or if paid options are worth exploring.

title: AI-Powered Research: Perplexity + NotebookLM
description: Use AI to research a topic of your choice, organize the findings, and generate an audio summary you can listen to.

toolsNeeded.role: Organize sources and generate audio summary

hardwareNeeded: Computer with web browser and speakers/headphones

steps.title: Research with Perplexity
steps.instructions: Go to perplexity.ai (free). Search for something you're genuinely curious about — e.g., 'What are the health benefits and risks of intermittent fasting based on recent studies?' Note how it cites sources for every claim.
steps.tip: Ask follow-up questions to go deeper — Perplexity remembers context within a thread.

steps.title: Save key sources
steps.instructions: From Perplexity's citations, open 3-5 of the most relevant source articles. Copy the URLs.

steps.title: Create a NotebookLM notebook
steps.instructions: Go to notebooklm.google.com (free, Google account needed). Click 'New Notebook'. Add sources: paste the URLs from step 2. NotebookLM will analyze them.
steps.tip: You can also upload PDFs, Google Docs, or paste text directly.

steps.title: Generate audio overview
steps.instructions: In NotebookLM, click 'Audio Overview' > 'Generate'. It will create a podcast-style discussion of your sources. Listen while doing other things!
steps.tip: The audio overview is surprisingly engaging — like two hosts discussing your research topic.

steps.title: Ask questions
steps.instructions: Use NotebookLM's chat to ask specific questions about your sources: 'What do the studies agree on?' or 'What are the main controversies?'
steps.tip: NotebookLM only answers from YOUR sources — no hallucination from outside data.

expectedOutcome: A well-organized research notebook with cited sources and an audio summary you can listen to. This workflow replaces hours of manual research.

title: DeepSeek vs ChatGPT: The Free Challenger
description: Test DeepSeek's free AI against ChatGPT. DeepSeek rivals top Western models at a fraction of the cost — see if the hype is real.

toolsNeeded.role: Free Chinese AI — strong at coding and math

steps.title: Prepare test prompts
steps.instructions: Use 2 prompts: (1) Coding: 'Write a Python script that downloads a webpage, extracts all links, and saves them to a CSV file. Include error handling.' (2) Reasoning: 'A farmer needs to cross a river with a wolf, a goat, and a cabbage. He can only take one at a time. The wolf eats the goat if left alone, the goat eats the cabbage. How does he do it? Explain step by step.'

steps.title: Test on DeepSeek
steps.instructions: Go to deepseek.com, create a free account. Send both prompts. Save the responses.
steps.tip: Try enabling 'Deep Think' mode for the reasoning problem — it shows the model's thought process.

steps.title: Test on ChatGPT
steps.instructions: Go to chat.openai.com (free). Send the same prompts. Save responses.

steps.title: Compare quality
steps.instructions: For the code: Which is cleaner? More complete? Better error handling? For reasoning: Which explained more clearly? Got the right answer faster?
steps.tip: DeepSeek often matches or beats ChatGPT on coding/math — the surprise is that it's completely free.

expectedOutcome: Direct comparison showing where DeepSeek matches ChatGPT and where it falls short. You may find a free alternative that works for most of your needs.

title: Your First AI Automation with Zapier
description: Create an automated workflow that uses AI to process information — e.g., summarize emails, auto-categorize tasks, or generate social posts.

steps.title: Sign up for Zapier
steps.instructions: Go to zapier.com and create a free account. You get 100 tasks/month free.

steps.title: Create a Zap
steps.instructions: Click 'Create Zap'. For a beginner automation: Trigger = 'New Email in Gmail (matching search)' — filter for newsletters or a specific sender. Action = 'AI by Zapier: Summarize text'. This will auto-summarize incoming emails.
steps.tip: Start with email summarization — it's immediately useful and easy to set up.

steps.title: Configure the AI step
steps.instructions: In the AI action, set the prompt to: 'Summarize this email in 2-3 bullet points. Include any action items.' Map the email body as input.
steps.tip: Zapier's built-in AI is powered by GPT — no separate API key needed.

steps.title: Add an output
steps.instructions: Add another action: save the summary to Google Sheets, Notion, or send yourself a Slack message. This creates a running log of summarized emails.
steps.tip: Google Sheets is the easiest output to start with.

steps.title: Test and activate
steps.instructions: Click 'Test' to run the Zap with a real email. Check the output. If it looks good, turn it on!
steps.tip: The free tier gives you 100 tasks/month — enough to automate 3-4 emails per day.

expectedOutcome: A working automation that saves you time every day. Once you see how easy it is, you'll want to automate more workflows.

title: Clone Your Voice with ElevenLabs
description: Create an AI clone of your voice that can read any text. Useful for narration, accessibility, or fun.
hardwareNeeded: Computer with web browser and microphone

steps.title: Sign up for ElevenLabs
steps.instructions: Go to elevenlabs.io and create a free account. You get 10,000 characters/month free.

steps.title: Record voice samples
steps.instructions: Go to 'Voices' > 'Add Voice' > 'Instant Voice Cloning'. Record yourself reading the provided sample text (about 30 seconds). Speak clearly and naturally.
steps.tip: A quiet room makes a huge difference in clone quality.

steps.title: Test your voice clone
steps.instructions: Go to 'Speech Synthesis'. Select your cloned voice. Type any text and click 'Generate'. Listen to your AI voice!
steps.tip: Try different text to see how well it captures your tone and cadence.

steps.title: Try different use cases
steps.instructions: Generate: (1) A professional narration for a presentation, (2) A fun message to surprise a friend, (3) Reading a long article so you can listen instead of read.
steps.tip: Voice cloning raises ethical questions — only clone your own voice or voices you have permission to use.

expectedOutcome: An AI clone of your voice that can read any text. You'll understand the power (and responsibility) of voice cloning technology.

title: Run AI Locally (No Internet, Full Privacy)
description: Run an AI chatbot entirely on your own computer. No data leaves your machine — complete privacy. Requires a decent GPU.

steps.title: Install Ollama
steps.instructions: Go to ollama.com and download the installer for your OS. Run the installer — it's a simple one-click setup.
steps.tip: Ollama makes running local AI as easy as running a chat app.

steps.title: Download a model
steps.instructions: Open a terminal/command prompt. Type: 'ollama pull llama3.2' (3B model, ~2GB). Wait for download. For better quality: 'ollama pull llama3.1' (8B model, ~5GB).
steps.tip: Start with the smaller model. You can always download larger ones later.

steps.title: Chat with local AI
steps.instructions: Type: 'ollama run llama3.2'. Start chatting! Everything runs on your machine — no internet needed after download.
steps.tip: It won't be as good as ChatGPT/Claude, but it's completely private and free forever.

steps.title: Try a web interface (optional)
steps.instructions: For a nicer interface, install Open WebUI: go to openwebui.com, follow the Docker or pip install instructions. This gives you a ChatGPT-like interface for your local models.
steps.tip: Open WebUI supports multiple models — download several and compare them locally.

expectedOutcome: A fully private AI running on your own hardware. Great for sensitive tasks or when you want AI without any data leaving your computer.

title: Text to Physical Object: AI 3D Printing Pipeline
description: Generate a 3D model from a text description using AI, repair the mesh, and prepare it for 3D printing. Test the full pipeline from idea to printable file.
hardwareNeeded: 3D printer OR online print service account (JLCPCB, Shapeways)

steps.title: Choose a test object
steps.instructions: Pick something simple but recognizable: a chess knight, a small vase, a phone stand, or a figurine. Use the same prompt for all 3 tools: 'A detailed chess knight piece, smooth surface, solid base, 5cm tall'
steps.tip: Simple objects with a flat base print easiest. Avoid overhangs and thin features for your first test.

steps.title: Generate in Meshy
steps.instructions: Go to meshy.ai, create a free account. Choose 'Text to 3D'. Enter your prompt. Wait for generation (~2-5 minutes). Export as STL or 3MF format.
steps.tip: 3MF is better than STL — it includes scale information and is more reliable for printing.

steps.title: Generate in Tripo and PrintPal
steps.instructions: Repeat with tripo3d.ai and printpal.io using the same prompt. Export STL/3MF from each. You now have 3 versions to compare.
steps.tip: Different generators excel at different shapes. Compare which produces the cleanest result for YOUR prompt.

steps.title: Check mesh quality
steps.instructions: Open each STL in an online mesh viewer (3dviewer.net is free). Look for: holes in the surface, floating fragments, walls that look paper-thin. If you have Blender (free), enable the 3D Print Toolbox add-on for detailed mesh analysis.
steps.tip: A 'manifold' mesh is watertight with no holes — this is what printers need. Non-manifold = print failure.

steps.title: Repair if needed
steps.instructions: If mesh has issues, use Meshmixer (free, Autodesk) or the Blender 3D Print Toolbox: select non-manifold edges, fill holes, remove loose parts. Or use 3D AI Studio's mesh repair beta.
steps.tip: Don't skip this step. Even the best AI generators produce non-watertight meshes 45% of the time.

steps.title: Scale and slice
steps.instructions: Open the repaired STL in your slicer (Bambu Studio, OrcaSlicer, or Cura — all free). Scale to your target size (e.g., 5cm tall). Check the preview — any red areas indicate printing problems. Slice and export G-code.
steps.tip: If you don't have a printer, JLCPCB offers 3D printing starting at ~$1. Upload your STL at jlcpcb.com/3d-printing.

steps.title: Print and evaluate
steps.instructions: Print the best version. Evaluate: surface quality, dimensional accuracy, structural strength. Take photos for your records. Compare against what you imagined from the text prompt.
steps.tip: Your first AI-generated print won't be perfect. The goal is understanding the pipeline — quality improves with better prompts and repair skills.

expectedOutcome: A physical 3D-printed object that started as a text description. You'll understand the full AI→print pipeline: what works, where it breaks, and how to fix it. This is the foundation for the Text-to-Physical-Object moonshot project.

title: AI-Designed PCB: From Description to Manufactured Board
description: Use AI tools to design a simple PCB — LED blinker or USB breakout board. Test Quilter and Flux.ai. Export Gerbers and order from JLCPCB. See if AI can replace manual PCB design for simple projects.

steps.title: Pick a simple project
steps.instructions: Start with an LED blinker using a 555 timer (classic beginner circuit) or a USB-C breakout board (useful and simple). These are 1-2 layer boards with <20 components.
steps.tip: Don't start with anything complex. The goal is testing AI tools, not designing a motherboard.

steps.title: Design schematic in Flux.ai
steps.instructions: Go to flux.ai, create an account. Use the AI copilot to help place components and wire the schematic. For a 555 blinker: the AI knows this circuit. Ask it.
steps.tip: Flux.ai's AI copilot can suggest components and connections, but YOU need to verify the schematic makes sense.

steps.title: Auto-layout with Quilter
steps.instructions: Export your schematic from Flux (or create in KiCad). Import into quilter.ai. Let Quilter auto-place and auto-route. Compare against manual layout if you know how.
steps.tip: Quilter is free during beta. It generates its first layout candidate in about an hour.

steps.title: Run DRC
steps.instructions: In Flux.ai, select the JLCPCB DRC template. Run Design Rule Check. Fix any violations (trace width too thin, spacing too close, drill sizes wrong).
steps.tip: JLCPCB's minimum trace width is 0.127mm (5mil). Minimum spacing is the same. Minimum drill is 0.3mm.

steps.title: Export and order
steps.instructions: Export Gerber files + drill files. Go to jlcpcb.com, upload Gerbers. Review the preview — does it look right? 5 boards cost ~$2-5. Order.
steps.tip: JLCPCB ships in 3-7 days. Check the Gerber preview carefully before ordering — mistakes cost time, not much money.

steps.title: Solder and test
steps.instructions: When boards arrive, solder components. Power up. Does it work? Document: what the AI got right, what you had to fix manually.
steps.tip: If it doesn't work, use a multimeter to check connections. AI-designed boards sometimes have subtle routing errors.

expectedOutcome: A working PCB that was primarily designed by AI. You'll understand what AI EDA tools can and can't do, and where human expertise is still needed. This is Phase 1 of the AI Electronics Lab moonshot.

title: 60-Second AI Short Film: Zero Camera, Zero Budget
description: Produce a complete 60-second short film using only AI — script, visuals, video, voice, music, editing. Two AI films premiered at Sundance 2026. A solo creator got a world record. You can do 60 seconds.
hardwareNeeded: Free video editor (DaVinci Resolve or CapCut)

steps.title: Write the script
steps.instructions: Ask ChatGPT or Claude: 'Write a 60-second short film script with narration. Genre: [sci-fi/mystery/comedy]. Include shot descriptions for 8-10 shots, each 5-8 seconds. Keep dialogue minimal — narration is easier for AI voice.'
steps.tip: Narration-driven films work much better than dialogue — you avoid the lip-sync problem entirely.

steps.title: Generate key frames
steps.instructions: For each shot, use Midjourney, DALL-E, or FLUX to generate the visual. Use consistent style prompts: add '--sref [URL]' in Midjourney for style reference, or describe the same art style in every prompt.
steps.tip: Character consistency is THE hardest part. Use a simple art style (illustration, watercolor, anime) — it's more forgiving than photorealistic.

steps.title: Animate with video AI
steps.instructions: Take each key frame image and upload to Runway Gen-4 (or Kling). Use 'Image to Video' mode. Generate 4-5 second clips for each shot. Download all clips.
steps.tip: Keep clips short (3-5 seconds). Longer generations lose coherence. You can always extend with slow-motion.

steps.title: Generate voice and music
steps.instructions: ElevenLabs: paste your narration text, choose a voice, generate audio. Suno: describe the mood ('cinematic ambient score, mysterious, slow build') and generate a 60-second track.
steps.tip: Generate 2-3 voice options and 2-3 music options. Pick the best combination.

steps.title: Assemble in editor
steps.instructions: Open DaVinci Resolve (free) or CapCut (free). Import all clips, arrange in order. Layer: video track + narration track + music track. Add transitions between shots. Adjust music volume under narration.
steps.tip: Sound design makes or breaks it. Music at 20-30% volume under narration. Add 0.5-second crossfades between shots.

steps.title: Export and publish
steps.instructions: Export at 1080p. Watch it. Be honest about quality. Upload to YouTube (unlisted if you prefer). Share with someone for feedback.
steps.tip: Your first one won't win Sundance. But you'll understand the entire pipeline and know exactly where to improve next time.

expectedOutcome: A complete 60-second film made entirely with AI. It won't be perfect — but it will exist, and you'll understand every step of the pipeline. This is the foundation for the AI Film Studio moonshot.

title: Multi-AI Chain: One Task, Five AIs, Compare Results
description: Give the same complex task to 5 different AIs. Then chain them: AI-1 writes, AI-2 critiques, AI-3 rewrites, AI-4 fact-checks, AI-5 formats. See what happens when AIs review each other's work.

steps.title: Pick a complex topic
steps.instructions: Choose something that requires accuracy and nuance: 'Write a 500-word analysis of the current state of AI regulation in Canada, with specific policy names, dates, and implications for small businesses.'
steps.tip: Pick a topic you care about and can verify. The point is seeing how AIs differ and where they fail.

steps.title: Round 1: Parallel generation
steps.instructions: Paste the same prompt into ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, Perplexity, and DeepSeek. Save all 5 responses. Don't read them yet.
steps.tip: All 5 have free tiers. If you haven't signed up for all of them yet, now's the time.

steps.title: Round 2: Cross-review
steps.instructions: Take ChatGPT's response and paste it into Claude with: 'Review this for accuracy, identify errors, suggest improvements, and rate it 1-10.' Do the same with Claude's response in ChatGPT.
steps.tip: AIs are surprisingly good at catching each other's mistakes. They're also sometimes wrong about what's wrong.

steps.title: Round 3: Fact-check
steps.instructions: Take the best response and paste it into Perplexity: 'Fact-check every claim in this text. For each claim, provide a source URL.' Compare against what you can verify yourself.
steps.tip: Perplexity cites sources — but check if those sources actually say what Perplexity claims they say.

steps.title: Round 4: Final synthesis
steps.instructions: Take the best elements from all responses. Give Claude (or your preferred AI) all 5 responses and ask: 'Synthesize these 5 perspectives into one definitive response. Flag any disagreements between the sources.'
steps.tip: The synthesized version is almost always better than any single AI's response. This is the power of multi-AI workflows.

steps.title: Score and document
steps.instructions: Rate each AI's original response for: accuracy (1-10), depth (1-10), readability (1-10), and uniqueness (1-10). Save results. This becomes your personal AI comparison database.
steps.tip: Your rankings will differ from published benchmarks because YOUR use case matters more than general benchmarks.

expectedOutcome: A clear understanding of how different AIs think, where each excels, and proof that multi-AI chains produce better results than any single AI. This is the foundation for the AI Orchestrator in the Idea Machine project.
